accreditation_agencies.definition_of_quality: A quality engineering program develops graduates who can apply scientific and mathematical principles to analyze, design, and solve complex, open-ended engineering problems while adhering to professional and ethical standards.
core_quality_dimensions: Impact of engineering on society and the environment
curriculum_requirements: Minimum 420 hours of mathematics and natural sciences
Minimum 900 hours of engineering science and engineering design
Substantial design experience throughout the program
Capstone design project with open-ended scope
Integration of indigenous perspectives in engineering practice
what_agencies_measure: Graduate attribute attainment per CEAB 12 attributes
Assessment and evaluation of graduate attributes
best_practices_for_programs: Spiral curriculum deepening competencies across semesters
Strong industry partnerships for co-ops and capstone

institutional_context.consolidated_summary: Lambton College is a public college of applied arts and technology located in Sarnia, Ontario, Canada. Founded in 1969, the institution serves approximately 11,000 students including a large international student population from over 90 countries. Lambton's mission centers on transforming lives through personalized, applied, and experiential learning. The College is known for its strong industry partnerships, particularly with the petrochemical sector in Sarnia, though its programs span health sciences, business, technology, and trades. Strategic priorities include sustainability, Indigenous education integration, digital transformation of learning, and community-responsive program development. The institution emphasizes academic quality through its commitment to applied research and work-integrated learning, with co-op placements embedded across many programs. Lambton's values include inclusion, respect, integrity, and collaboration, which are embedded in curriculum design guidelines.

reputation.summary: Lambton College enjoys a positive reputation in Ontario's college sector, particularly for its personalized student experience and strong graduate employment outcomes. Reviews from students on platforms such as Rate My Professors and Google highlight the accessibility of faculty and the practical, hands-on nature of the curriculum. The College has received recognition for its international student support services and its work-integrated learning programs. Employer surveys indicate high satisfaction with Lambton graduates, citing readiness for immediate employment and strong professional attitudes. Some reviews note that the campus's location in Sarnia, while a smaller city, contributes to a tight-knit community atmosphere but may limit extracurricular opportunities compared to larger urban institutions. Lambton's partnerships with industry leaders in chemical processing and technology have translated into strong co-op placement rates exceeding 85% in recent years. The institution's reputation in the technology sector is growing, supported by investments in computing labs, cloud infrastructure access, and cybersecurity training facilities.

legal_framework.answer: In Ontario, degree-granting authority for colleges is governed by the Post-secondary Education Choice and Excellence Act (PSECE Act, 2000). Lambton College must obtain consent from the Minister of Colleges and Universities to offer applied bachelor's degrees. The consent process requires a thorough self-study, a site visit, and peer review by the Postsecondary Education Quality Assessment Board (PEQAB). PEQAB standards require that applied bachelor degrees demonstrate: a minimum of 40% content at the upper-division level (years 3-4), adherence to the Qualifications Framework for Ontario, integration of work-integrated learning, and a research or capstone component. Federal regulations (via IRCC) also affect international student recruitment, with PGWP eligibility tied to DLI designation and program approval status. The College must maintain Ontario Student Assistance Program (OSAP) eligibility by meeting annual performance standards. Additionally, AODA (Accessibility for Ontarians with Disabilities Act) compliance is mandatory for all learning materials and delivery modes.

competitive_landscape.answer: The applied software engineering degree space in Ontario is competitive. Key competitors include: Sheridan College (Honours Bachelor of Applied Computer Science), Conestoga College (Honours Bachelor of Software Development), and Seneca College (Honours Bachelor of Information Technology). Each of these programs positions itself on co-op integration, industry partnerships, and specialized tracks (AI, cybersecurity, game development). The University of Waterloo's Computer Science program remains the aspirational benchmark for academically stronger students. Lambton's differentiation opportunity lies in its smaller cohort sizes (enabling personalized mentorship), its location advantage for industry placement in the Sarnia industrial corridor, and its proven track record in applied learning. Salary data from LinkedIn and Indeed for Lambton graduates in technology roles ranges from $58,000 to $72,000 at entry level — competitive with peer institutions. The market shows strong demand growth, with Ontario technology job postings increasing 18% year-over-year. Gaps in competitors' programs include insufficient coverage of cloud-native development and AI/ML integration, which represent areas for Lambton to differentiate.

learning_outcomes.markdown:
# Program Overview

The Bachelor of Applied Software Engineering at Lambton College prepares graduates for immediate professional impact in Canada's growing technology sector. The program bridges theoretical computer science foundations with the applied, project-based learning that distinguishes Lambton's educational model. Students engage with real-world problems from the Sarnia industrial corridor and beyond, developing the technical depth and professional agility demanded by modern software employers.

Anchored in Lambton's values of inclusion, integrity, and collaboration, the program integrates ethical reasoning, accessibility standards, and sustainable development practices throughout all four years. A mandatory co-op placement in Year 3 and a capstone project in Year 4 provide graduates with portfolio-ready artifacts and professional network connections before convocation.

The program is designed for students who want a career-ready degree that combines the rigor of university-level software engineering with the applied, employer-connected approach that has made Lambton College graduates consistently sought after in the Ontario labour market.

# Program Educational Objectives (PEOs)

1. Graduates will design, build, and maintain software systems that solve complex, open-ended problems in professional engineering and technology environments.
2. Graduates will apply sound engineering principles — including requirements analysis, testing, and continuous improvement — throughout the full software development lifecycle.
3. Graduates will contribute as effective team members and emerging leaders, communicating clearly with technical and non-technical stakeholders across multidisciplinary teams.
4. Graduates will practise their profession with integrity, guided by ethical frameworks, equity considerations, and respect for the societal and environmental impact of software systems.
5. Graduates will commit to continuous professional development, adapting their skills as technology evolves.

# Student Learning Outcomes (SLOs)

1. **Computational Foundations** — Apply mathematical, statistical, and algorithmic reasoning to analyze and solve software engineering problems.
2. **Software Design & Architecture** — Design scalable, maintainable software systems using established architectural patterns and design principles.
3. **Full-Stack Development** — Build functional, accessible web and cloud-native applications across front-end and back-end layers.
4. **Data Management** — Design and query relational and non-relational databases; apply data modelling and integrity principles.
5. **DevOps & Cloud Engineering** — Implement CI/CD pipelines, containerization, and infrastructure-as-code on major cloud platforms.
6. **AI & Data Intelligence** — Apply machine learning concepts and data analysis techniques to build intelligent, data-driven software features.
7. **Cybersecurity Practices** — Identify common vulnerabilities and apply security-by-design principles throughout the software development lifecycle.
8. **Professional & Agile Practice** — Plan, estimate, and deliver software iteratively using Agile/Scrum frameworks within team environments.
9. **Communication & Documentation** — Produce clear technical documentation, reports, and presentations for diverse audiences.
10. **Ethics & Societal Responsibility** — Evaluate the ethical, legal, and social implications of software products and make responsible engineering decisions.
11. **Applied Research & Innovation** — Investigate emerging technologies through structured inquiry and apply findings to novel engineering problems.
12. **Capstone Integration** — Independently design, build, test, and present a substantial software system that addresses a real-world problem and integrates program-wide competencies.

# Graduate Profile

Lambton's Bachelor of Applied Software Engineering graduate is a collaborative, technically fluent engineer ready to contribute from day one. They bring strong Python, SQL, cloud, and API development skills, complemented by professional competencies in communication, ethics, and teamwork. They have completed a professional co-op placement and a portfolio-quality capstone project. They are equipped for roles such as Software Developer, Full-Stack Engineer, Cloud Developer, or Junior AI/ML Engineer, and are positioned for progression toward senior technical and leadership roles within five years of graduation.

course_list.markdown:
## Year 1 — Semester 1

**[CS101] Introduction to Software Engineering** (3 credits) — Core
> Foundational principles of software engineering: lifecycle models, requirements, design, testing, and professional ethics. Addresses SLOs: #1, #10.

**[CS102] Programming Fundamentals with Python** (3 credits) — Core
> Variables, control flow, functions, and object-oriented programming in Python with emphasis on readable, testable code. Addresses SLOs: #1, #3.

**[MATH101] Discrete Mathematics** (3 credits) — Core
> Logic, sets, graph theory, combinatorics, and proof techniques as applied to computing. Addresses SLOs: #1.

**[CS103] Web Development Fundamentals** (3 credits) — Core
> HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and responsive design; introduction to the browser rendering model and accessibility standards. Addresses SLOs: #3, #10.

**[COMM101] Technical Communication** (3 credits) — Core
> Writing reports, user documentation, and technical proposals; oral presentation skills for engineering audiences. Addresses SLOs: #9.

## Year 1 — Semester 2

**[CS104] Data Structures & Algorithms** (3 credits) — Core
> Arrays, linked lists, trees, graphs, sorting, and searching; time and space complexity analysis. Addresses SLOs: #1, #2.

**[CS105] Relational Databases** (3 credits) — Core
> Entity-relationship modelling, SQL, normalization, transactions, and database administration basics. Addresses SLOs: #4.

**[CS106] Object-Oriented Design** (3 credits) — Core
> Design patterns, UML, SOLID principles, and refactoring in Python and Java. Addresses SLOs: #2, #3.

**[MATH102] Statistics for Software Engineers** (3 credits) — Core
> Probability distributions, hypothesis testing, regression, and data-driven decision making. Addresses SLOs: #1, #6.

**[CS107] Operating Systems & Linux** (3 credits) — Core
> Processes, memory management, file systems, and shell scripting in Linux/Unix environments. Addresses SLOs: #1, #5.

## Year 2 — Semester 3

**[CS201] Software Requirements & Modelling** (3 credits) — Core
> Use-case analysis, user stories, requirements elicitation, and modelling with UML. Addresses SLOs: #2, #8.

**[CS202] Full-Stack Web Development** (3 credits) — Core
> React, RESTful APIs, Node.js/FastAPI backends, authentication, and deployment. Addresses SLOs: #3, #5.

**[CS203] Cloud Computing Fundamentals** (3 credits) — Core
> AWS/Azure/GCP core services: compute, storage, networking, IAM, and cost management. Addresses SLOs: #5.

**[CS204] Agile Software Development** (3 credits) — Core
> Scrum, Kanban, sprint planning, retrospectives, and team dynamics in software projects. Addresses SLOs: #8, #9.

**[CS205] Networks & APIs** (3 credits) — Core
> TCP/IP, HTTP/HTTPS, REST and GraphQL API design, security headers, and rate limiting. Addresses SLOs: #3, #7.

## Year 2 — Semester 4

**[CS206] Advanced Databases** (3 credits) — Core
> NoSQL systems (MongoDB, Redis), query optimization, replication, and polyglot persistence. Addresses SLOs: #4.

**[CS207] Software Testing & Quality** (3 credits) — Core
> Unit, integration, and end-to-end testing; TDD, BDD, coverage analysis, and test automation frameworks. Addresses SLOs: #2, #8.

**[CS208] DevOps & CI/CD** (3 credits) — Core
> Git workflows, GitHub Actions/GitLab CI, Docker, containerization, and automated deployment pipelines. Addresses SLOs: #5, #8.

**[CS209] Introduction to Machine Learning** (3 credits) — Core
> Supervised and unsupervised learning, model evaluation, scikit-learn, and introductory neural networks. Addresses SLOs: #6.

**[CS210] Cybersecurity Fundamentals** (3 credits) — Core
> OWASP Top 10, threat modelling, encryption, secure coding practices, and penetration testing basics. Addresses SLOs: #7.

## Year 3 — Semester 5 (Co-op)

**[COOP301] Professional Co-op Placement I** (15 credits) — Core
> Supervised work-integrated learning placement in a technology organization; reflective journals and employer evaluation. Addresses SLOs: #8, #9, #10.

## Year 3 — Semester 6

**[CS301] Cloud-Native Architecture** (3 credits) — Core
> Microservices, event-driven design, Kubernetes, service mesh, and infrastructure as code with Terraform. Addresses SLOs: #2, #5.

**[CS302] Data Engineering & Pipelines** (3 credits) — Core
> ETL/ELT, Apache Spark basics, data lakes, streaming data, and pipeline orchestration. Addresses SLOs: #4, #6.

**[CS303] Applied Machine Learning** (3 credits) — Core
> Deep learning with PyTorch/TensorFlow, NLP basics, model deployment, and MLOps. Addresses SLOs: #6, #11.

**[CS304] System Design & Scalability** (3 credits) — Core
> Distributed systems, caching, load balancing, consistency models, and trade-off analysis for large-scale applications. Addresses SLOs: #2, #5.

**[ENGL301] Professional Ethics & Indigenous Perspectives** (3 credits) — Core
> Engineering ethics frameworks, Indigenous data sovereignty, environmental impact of software, and responsible AI. Addresses SLOs: #10.

## Year 4 — Semester 7

**[CS401] Generative AI & LLM Integration** (3 credits) — Elective
> Prompt engineering, Retrieval-Augmented Generation, fine-tuning APIs, and deploying AI-powered applications. Addresses SLOs: #6, #11.

**[CS402] Mobile & Cross-Platform Development** (3 credits) — Elective
> React Native / Flutter; mobile UX, offline-first design, and app store deployment. Addresses SLOs: #3.

**[CS403] Applied Research Methods** (3 credits) — Core
> Research design, literature review, experimentation, and academic writing for applied computing. Addresses SLOs: #11.

**[CS404] Capstone Project — Design & Specification** (3 credits) — Capstone
> Problem identification, stakeholder analysis, system design, and project planning for the Year 4 capstone. Addresses SLOs: #2, #8, #12.

**[CS405] Observability & Site Reliability Engineering** (3 credits) — Elective
> Logging, metrics, distributed tracing, SLOs/SLAs, incident response, and chaos engineering. Addresses SLOs: #5, #7.

## Year 4 — Semester 8

**[CS406] Capstone Project — Build & Delivery** (6 credits) — Capstone
> Full implementation, testing, documentation, and public presentation of the capstone software system. Addresses SLOs: #2, #3, #8, #9, #11, #12.

**[CS407] Software Project Management** (3 credits) — Core
> Project estimation, risk management, stakeholder communication, contracts, and team leadership. Addresses SLOs: #8, #9, #10.

**[CS408] Emerging Technologies Seminar** (3 credits) — Elective
> Guest lectures and structured inquiry into current trends: quantum computing, edge AI, Web3, and future skills for engineers. Addresses SLOs: #11.

# Curriculum Threads

**Thread 1 — Software Engineering Core (CS101 → CS106 → CS201 → CS207 → CS404 → CS406)**
Establishes engineering discipline from first principles through design, testing, and capstone delivery. Students encounter professional software practice in Year 1, deepen it through testing and requirements in Year 2, apply it in co-op in Year 3, and demonstrate mastery in the Year 4 capstone.

**Thread 2 — Cloud & Infrastructure (CS107 → CS203 → CS208 → CS301 → CS405)**
Builds from operating system foundations to cloud fundamentals, DevOps automation, cloud-native architecture, and production observability. Reflects the industry reality that software engineers must own deployment and reliability, not just code.

**Thread 3 — Data & Intelligence (MATH102 → CS105 → CS206 → CS302 → CS209 → CS303 → CS401)**
Progresses from statistical reasoning and relational data through advanced databases, data engineering, machine learning, and generative AI. Positions graduates to build data-driven features across a wide range of application domains.

**Thread 4 — Professional Practice & Ethics (COMM101 → CS204 → COOP301 → ENGL301 → CS407)**
Weaves communication, Agile practice, real-world co-op, Indigenous and ethical perspectives, and project management across all four years. Ensures graduates enter the workforce as ethically grounded, collaborative professionals.

**Thread 5 — Applied Research & Innovation (CS403 → CS401 → CS408 → CS406)**
Cultivates a research mindset through structured inquiry, emerging technology exploration, and an independent capstone — preparing graduates who can navigate and shape future technology rather than merely consume it.

# Assessment Philosophy

Assessment in the Bachelor of Applied Software Engineering is outcomes-referenced and portfolio-building by design. Each course maps its assessments directly to program SLOs, using a combination of formative tasks (weekly labs, code reviews, sprint demos) and summative deliverables (design documents, deployed applications, examinations). The competency map guides the sequence of I → D → A so that students encounter each outcome multiple times before it is formally assessed, ensuring mastery rather than surface exposure.

The program closes the assessment loop through two mechanisms: an annual curriculum committee review that analyzes aggregate SLO attainment data from course-level rubrics, and an employer advisory council that validates whether graduate competencies match evolving industry expectations. Results inform course revision, new elective development, and co-op placement strategy — embodying Lambton's commitment to continuous improvement in applied, responsive education.

CS102.markdown:
# CS102: Programming Fundamentals with Python

## Course Description
This course introduces students to computational thinking and programming through the Python language. Emphasis is placed on writing clean, readable, and well-tested code from the first week, establishing habits that will serve students throughout the entire program and their professional careers.

## Prerequisites
None.

## Course Learning Outcomes
1. Write syntactically correct Python programs using variables, data types, operators, and control flow structures.
2. Design and implement functions that decompose problems into reusable, testable units.
3. Apply object-oriented programming principles — encapsulation, inheritance, and polymorphism — to model real-world entities in code.
4. Read, trace, and debug Python programs systematically using print statements, assertions, and an IDE debugger.
5. Use lists, dictionaries, and file I/O to store, retrieve, and process structured data.
6. Write basic unit tests using the `pytest` framework to verify the correctness of functions and classes.

## Weekly Schedule
| Week | Topic | Learning Activities | Assessment/Deliverable |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Introduction to Python & the REPL | Live coding, environment setup lab | Lab 1: Hello World + arithmetic |
| 2 | Variables, types, and expressions | Pair programming exercises | Lab 2: Type conversion quiz |
| 3 | Control flow: if/elif/else | Case study: grade classifier | Lab 3: GPA calculator |
| 4 | Loops: for and while | Algorithm tracing exercises | Lab 4: Number guessing game |
| 5 | Functions: parameters and return values | Refactoring workshop | **Assignment 1 due** (calculator app) |
| 6 | Scope, recursion, and the call stack | Stack trace visualisation | Lab 5: Recursive fibonacci |
| 7 | Lists, tuples, and slicing | Collaborative data challenge | Lab 6: Student grade list |
| 8 | **Midterm Exam** | Review session | Midterm: Weeks 1–7 content |
| 9 | Dictionaries and sets | Real-world JSON exploration | Lab 7: Word frequency counter |
| 10 | File I/O and CSV processing | Industry dataset lab | Lab 8: CSV data analyser |
| 11 | Introduction to OOP: classes and objects | UML mini-activity | Lab 9: BankAccount class |
| 12 | Inheritance and polymorphism | Design exercise | Lab 10: Animal hierarchy |
| 13 | Error handling: exceptions and debugging | Bug hunt lab | **Assignment 2 due** (OOP mini-project) |
| 14 | Introduction to testing with pytest | TDD red-green-refactor demo | Lab 11: Write tests for Assignment 2 |
| 15 | Code quality: PEP 8, linting, type hints | Code review workshop | Lab 12: Refactor and lint |
| 16 | Final Project presentations | Peer review + Q&A | **Final Project due** |

## Assessment Plan
| Component | Weight | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Weekly Labs (12 × ~1%) | 12% | Hands-on in-class coding exercises, auto-graded via pytest |
| Assignment 1 — Calculator App | 15% | Command-line app using functions and control flow |
| Assignment 2 — OOP Mini-Project | 20% | Class-based program modelling a real-world entity |
| Midterm Exam | 18% | Written + coding exam covering Weeks 1–7 |
| Final Project | 25% | Individual Python application with tests, documentation, and live demo |
| Participation | 10% | Engagement in labs, peer review, and in-class activities |
| **Total** | **100%** | |

## Required Resources
- Matthes, E. (2023). *Python Crash Course* (3rd ed.). No Starch Press.
- Python 3.11+ (free): https://www.python.org/downloads/
- VS Code with Python extension (free): https://code.visualstudio.com/
- Course GitHub Classroom (link provided Week 1)

## Teaching Methodology
This course uses a flipped-classroom model: short video lectures are assigned before class, and in-class time is devoted entirely to live coding, debugging labs, and peer programming. Weekly labs are completed in pairs using driver-navigator rotation to build collaboration habits. The instructor conducts live code reviews using student submissions (with permission) to normalize feedback and iterative improvement from the first week.

## Grading Scale
| Grade | Range |
|---|---|
| A+ | 90–100% |
| A | 85–89% |
| A- | 80–84% |
| B+ | 77–79% |
| B | 73–76% |
| B- | 70–72% |
| C+ | 67–69% |
| C | 63–66% |
| C- | 60–62% |
| D | 50–59% |
| F | Below 50% |

CS208.markdown:
# CS208: DevOps & CI/CD

## Course Description
This course equips students with the tools, practices, and mindset of modern DevOps engineering. Students build fully automated delivery pipelines — from code commit to production deployment — using industry-standard toolchains including Git, Docker, GitHub Actions, and cloud infrastructure. The course bridges software development and operations, reflecting current employer expectations that engineers own the full delivery lifecycle.

## Prerequisites
CS107 Operating Systems & Linux; CS106 Object-Oriented Design.

## Course Learning Outcomes
1. Manage a professional Git workflow including branching strategies, pull requests, code review, and merge conflict resolution.
2. Design and implement CI/CD pipelines using GitHub Actions that automate testing, building, and deploying software.
3. Containerize applications using Docker and orchestrate multi-container environments with Docker Compose.
4. Deploy containerized applications to a cloud platform (AWS or Azure) using managed container services.
5. Apply infrastructure-as-code principles using Terraform to provision and manage cloud resources reproducibly.
6. Configure monitoring, logging, and alerting for deployed applications to detect and respond to production incidents.

## Weekly Schedule
| Week | Topic | Learning Activities | Assessment/Deliverable |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | DevOps principles & the CALMS framework | Case study: Netflix DevOps evolution | Lab 1: Git branching strategy |
| 2 | Advanced Git: branching, rebasing, and PRs | Hands-on Git workflow lab | Lab 2: Team PR simulation |
| 3 | Introduction to Docker: images and containers | Container build workshop | Lab 3: Dockerise a Python app |
| 4 | Docker Compose: multi-container apps | Full-stack app containerisation | Lab 4: App + database compose file |
| 5 | CI fundamentals: automated testing in pipelines | GitHub Actions walkthrough | **Assignment 1 due** (Docker + Compose) |
| 6 | Continuous delivery: build, test, and artifact pipelines | Pipeline design workshop | Lab 5: CI pipeline for a web app |
| 7 | Cloud deployment: AWS ECS / Azure Container Apps | Guided cloud lab | Lab 6: Deploy container to cloud |
| 8 | **Midterm Exam** | Pipeline review session | Midterm: Weeks 1–7 |
| 9 | Infrastructure as Code: Terraform fundamentals | Terraform hands-on lab | Lab 7: Provision EC2 + S3 with Terraform |
| 10 | Terraform modules, state, and remote backends | Pair Terraform project | Lab 8: Modular Terraform |
| 11 | Secrets management and security in pipelines | SAST/DAST integration demo | **Assignment 2 due** (full CI/CD pipeline) |
| 12 | Monitoring & observability: Prometheus + Grafana | Dashboard build lab | Lab 9: Instrument a deployed app |
| 13 | Logging pipelines: ELK/OpenTelemetry | Log aggregation exercise | Lab 10: Centralised logging |
| 14 | Incident response: runbooks and postmortems | Chaos engineering simulation | Lab 11: Inject failure, write postmortem |
| 15 | Capstone pipeline design reviews | Peer critique session | Pipeline architecture document |
| 16 | Final Project demos | Live pipeline demonstration | **Final Project due** |

## Assessment Plan
| Component | Weight | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Weekly Labs (11 × ~1%) | 11% | Hands-on pipeline labs submitted via GitHub |
| Assignment 1 — Dockerised App | 15% | Multi-container application with Docker Compose |
| Assignment 2 — Full CI/CD Pipeline | 25% | End-to-end pipeline: lint → test → build → deploy to cloud |
| Midterm Exam | 15% | Conceptual and scenario-based questions on Weeks 1–7 |
| Final Project | 30% | Production-grade pipeline with IaC, monitoring, and incident runbook |
| Participation | 4% | Lab engagement and peer review contributions |
| **Total** | **100%** | |

## Required Resources
- Kim, G., Humble, J., Debois, P., & Willis, J. (2021). *The DevOps Handbook* (2nd ed.). IT Revolution Press.
- Docker Desktop (free for students): https://www.docker.com/products/docker-desktop/
- GitHub account with GitHub Actions (free tier)
- AWS Academy or Azure for Students cloud account (provisioned by institution)
- Terraform CLI (free): https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/downloads

## Teaching Methodology
All lectures are demo-driven — every concept is shown live in a real terminal and pipeline dashboard. Students replicate the demo in their own environment during the same class session. Assignments are real projects, not toy exercises: students deploy actual applications to real cloud infrastructure and present a working pipeline to peers. Pair and team work is encouraged; all code is submitted via Git repositories, mirroring professional practice.

## Grading Scale
| Grade | Range |
|---|---|
| A+ | 90–100% |
| A | 85–89% |
| A- | 80–84% |
| B+ | 77–79% |
| B | 73–76% |
| B- | 70–72% |
| C+ | 67–69% |
| C | 63–66% |
| C- | 60–62% |
| D | 50–59% |
| F | Below 50% |
